content:
# Matthew Chapter 26 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 26 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 26:2 — "Passover" vs. "Pascha"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Pascha comes" | Preserves the Greek/Hebrew transliteration Πάσχα |
| LOGOS | "the Passover is coming" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Passover begins in two days" | Dynamic phrasing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Passover" (LOGOS). This is the standard English rendering of Πάσχα. EMET's "Pascha" preserves the Greek form but is unfamiliar to most English readers. PNEUMA restructures the sentence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Project-wide convention for Πάσχα.

---

## 26:7 — "ointment" vs. "perfumed-oil" vs. "perfume" for μύρου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "very costly perfumed-oil" | Compound literal rendering of μύρου βαρυτίμου |
| LOGOS | "very expensive ointment" | Formal — "ointment" for μύρον |
| PNEUMA | "expensive perfume" | Dynamic — modern term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "ointment" (LOGOS). μύρον was a costly aromatic oil/unguent used for anointing. "Ointment" is the traditional biblical rendering and captures the semi-solid nature of the substance. "Perfume" (PNEUMA) suggests a modern fragrance product. EMET's "perfumed-oil" is descriptive but hyphenated compounds are awkward.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 26:10 — "a beautiful thing" vs. "a good work" vs. "something wonderful" for καλὸν ἔργον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she worked a good work toward me" | Literal — καλόν as "good," ἔργον as "work" |
| LOGOS | "she has done a beautiful thing to me" | Formal — καλόν rendered as "beautiful" |
| PNEUMA | "She has done something wonderful for me" | Dynamic — elevated emotional register |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a beautiful thing" (LOGOS). The Greek καλός means both "good" and "beautiful." In this context — an extravagant act of devotion that the disciples dismiss as waste — "beautiful" captures the aesthetic and moral quality that Jesus affirms. EMET's "good work" is flat. PNEUMA's "wonderful" is vague.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Beautiful" captures the dual sense of καλός.

---

## 26:25 — "You have said so" / "You said [it]" — σὺ εἶπας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You said [it]" | Ultra-literal rendering of σὺ εἶπας |
| LOGOS | "You have said so" | Formal English rendering with perfect tense |
| PNEUMA | "You have said it yourself" | Dynamic — adds "yourself" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "You have said so" (LOGOS). This enigmatic formula is central to Matthew's Passion (used at both v.25 with Judas and v.64 with Caiaphas). LOGOS preserves the ambiguity — it is an affirmation that places the declaration back on the speaker. PNEUMA's "yourself" adds emphasis not in the Greek. EMET's rendering is too compressed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The formula must be consistent at both occurrences.

---

## 26:28 — "blood of the covenant" — Sinaiticus reading without "new"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the blood of me of the covenant" | Literal, no "new"; awkward English genitive |
| LOGOS | "my blood of the covenant" | Formal, no "new"; cleaner English |
| PNEUMA | "my blood of the covenant" | Dynamic, no "new"; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading, which lacks "new" (καινῆς) before "covenant." SOPHIA adopted LOGOS phrasing. This is a critical textual point: the Sinaiticus reading directly echoes Exodus 24:8 ("the blood of the covenant"), linking the Last Supper to the Sinai covenant without the later "new covenant" addition.

**Status:** Full agreement on textual basis. Resolved in favor of LOGOS for phrasing.

---

## 26:37 — "sorrowful and deeply troubled" vs. "sorrowful and troubled" vs. "overwhelmed with grief"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he began to be grieved and distressed" | Literal — λυπεῖσθαι καὶ ἀδημονεῖν |
| LOGOS | "he began to be sorrowful and troubled" | Formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "he became overwhelmed with grief" | Dynamic — combines both verbs into one intensified expression |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "sorrowful and deeply troubled" (modified from LOGOS). The Greek uses two distinct verbs — λυπεῖσθαι (to be grieved/sorrowful) and ἀδημονεῖν (to be distressed/deeply troubled). PNEUMA's single expression "overwhelmed with grief" collapses the distinction. Jesus experiences both sorrow and a deeper distress — the two verbs should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved with modification. Both emotional states are preserved.

---

## 26:38 — "My soul is very sorrowful, even to death" vs. "My soul is crushed with grief to the point of death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The soul of me is deeply grieved to [the point of] death" | Literal with ψυχή as "soul" |
| LOGOS | "My soul is very sorrowful, even to death" | Formal, echoing Psalm 42:5-6, 11 |
| PNEUMA | "My soul is crushed with grief to the point of death" | Dynamic — "crushed" intensifies beyond the Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. The phrase echoes Psalm 42-43 (the soul in anguish), and the formal rendering preserves this allusion. PNEUMA's "crushed" is emotionally powerful but adds intensity beyond what the Greek περίλυπος (deeply sorrowful) warrants.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 26:50 — "Friend, do what you came to do" — grammatical ambiguity

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Friend, [do] that for which you are here" | Imperative reading, preserving ambiguity markers |
| LOGOS | "Friend, do what you came to do" | Clean imperative reading |
| PNEUMA | "Friend, do what you came to do" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. The Greek ἐφ' ὃ πάρει is grammatically ambiguous — it could be a question ("Why are you here?") or a command ("Do what you came for"). SOPHIA follows the imperative reading: Jesus is not asking a question but giving Judas permission to complete his act. The narrative context (Jesus has already identified the betrayer) supports this.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Imperative reading adopted.

---

## 26:50 — "Friend" (ἑταῖρε) — the formal/cold "friend"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Friend" (notes ἑταῖρε vs. φίλος) | Preserves the word with textual note |
| LOGOS | "Friend" | Same, with note on the formula |
| PNEUMA | "Friend" (notes the cold formality vs. warm φίλος) | Same word, with interpretive note |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three render ἑταῖρε as "Friend." The critical distinction — that this is the formal, almost cold ἑταῖρε rather than the warm φίλος — is noted by EMET and PNEUMA. SOPHIA preserves "Friend" with the council note that this is the same word used for the man without a wedding garment (22:12) and the grumbling worker (20:13).

**Status:** Full agreement on rendering. Notes from EMET and PNEUMA incorporated.

---

## 26:64 — "right hand of Power" vs. "right hand of the Mighty One"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "at [the] right of the Power" | Literal — τῆς δυνάμεως as "the Power" |
| LOGOS | "at the right hand of Power" | Formal — "Power" capitalized as divine title |
| PNEUMA | "at the right hand of the Mighty One" | Dynamic — interprets the circumlocution |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "right hand of Power" (LOGOS). "The Power" (ἡ δύναμις) is a Jewish circumlocution for God — avoiding the divine name. LOGOS preserves this circumlocution with the capital P. PNEUMA's "Mighty One" interprets the circumlocution but loses the specific Jewish reverential practice.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The divine circumlocution is preserved.

---

## 26:71 — "Nazareth" vs. "Nazoraian" for Ναζωραίου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua the Nazoraian" | Preserves Sinaiticus form Ναζωραίου |
| LOGOS | "Jesus of Nazareth" | Standard English identification |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus of Nazareth" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "of Nazareth" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Sinaiticus reads Ναζωραίου, which EMET correctly notes may carry additional overtones (connection to נֵצֶר "branch" in Isaiah 11:1, or the Nazirite vow). However, for the narrative context — a servant girl identifying Peter's companion — "of Nazareth" is the natural English rendering. EMET's insight is preserved in the council notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's etymological note retained.

---

## 26:73 — "your accent betrays you" vs. "your speech makes you evident"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the speech of you makes you evident" | Literal — ἡ λαλιά σου δῆλόν σε ποιεῖ |
| LOGOS | "your accent betrays you" | Formal English with "accent" for λαλιά |
| PNEUMA | "your accent gives you away" | Dynamic, same idea |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "your accent betrays you" (LOGOS). λαλιά means "speech" or "way of speaking" — the Galilean accent was distinctive. "Accent betrays you" is vivid English that captures the dramatic irony: Peter's speech patterns reveal him even as his words deny Jesus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Effective English rendering.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA emotional intensification beyond the Greek | 3 | Greek intensity preserved without amplification |
| Vocabulary precision (ointment, Power, accent) | 3 | Most precise term adopted |
| Proper noun/transliteration conventions | 2 | English conventions |
| Textual fidelity (covenant without "new") | 1 | Full agreement on Sinaiticus reading |
| Grammatical ambiguity resolution | 1 | Imperative reading adopted |
| Enigmatic formula preservation (σὺ εἶπας) | 1 | Ambiguity maintained |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 26 — the beginning of the Passion Narrative — demands the highest level of translational precision. The most theologically charged divergences involve (1) the "beautiful thing" / "good work" question in v.10, where the dual meaning of καλός must be honored; (2) the "blood of the covenant" in v.28, where all three correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading without "new"; and (3) the enigmatic σὺ εἶπας formula in v.25/64. PNEUMA's tendency to intensify emotional language (v.37 "overwhelmed," v.38 "crushed") is understandable given the Passion context but risks going beyond what the Greek actually says. SOPHIA preserves the text's own emotional register, which is devastating precisely because of its restraint.

---

*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, Bible Translation Project*
